text: Increasing running step rate by 5% above preferred reduces mechanical energy absorption at the knee joint during loading response.
Increasing running step rate by 10% above preferred reduces mechanical energy absorption at both the knee and hip joints during loading response.
Reducing step rate by 10% below preferred substantially increases energy absorption at all lower extremity joints, increasing loading and potential injury risk.
Increasing step rate at constant speed reduces step length, center of mass vertical excursion, braking impulse, and peak knee flexion angle.

A 10% increase in step rate above preferred reduces peak hip adduction angle and peak hip adduction and internal rotation moments, which are associated with common running injuries like IT band syndrome.
Subtle increases in step rate (5-10% above preferred) can be used as a strategy to reduce lower extremity loading and may help prevent and treat common running-related injuries.
